title: Requests, Limits, QoS, Probes, Startup, Readiness, and Resource Failure · Reconciliation Brief: K8s Requests Limits Scheduling Runtime
summary: A slow-starting API is killed by liveness checks, serves traffic before warmup, and competes for memory without meaningful requests or limits. Planned artifact: Add and verify the k8s requests limits scheduling runtime increment in health and resource behavior policy. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Relate CPU and memory requests to scheduling and accounting, limits to runtime enforcement, and application concurrency to measured capacity.
Design short-lived credentials, audience, rotation, revocation, redaction, audit, reload, failure, and recovery evidence across workload and external systems.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# kubernetes-basics cumulative orchestration portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.
# They never invoke kubectl, read kubeconfig, contact a cluster API, or execute host commands.
workspace: kubernetes
version-baseline:
  kubernetes: 1.36.2
  minikube: 1.38.1
  gateway-api: 1.5.1
  helm: 4.2.2
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-cluster

steps.title: Predict K8s Requests Limits Scheduling Runtime · Theory K8s Requests Limits Scheduling Runtime
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory service passes liveness while serving stale data, has no startup probe, requests 100m but uses 1000m CPU, and is OOMKilled under pressure. Redesign probes, requests, limits, QoS, graceful termination, and changed-load checks. Competency probe: Relate CPU and memory requests to scheduling and accounting, limits to runtime enforcement, and application concurrency to measured capacity.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: A worked example service passes liveness while serving stale data, has no startup probe, requests 100m but uses 1000m CPU, and is OOMKilled under pressure. Redesign probes, requests, limits, QoS, graceful termination, and changed-load checks. Competency probe: Relate CPU and memory requests to scheduling and accounting, limits to runtime enforcement, and application concurrency to measured capacity. A defensible solution applies K8s Requests Limits Scheduling Runtime, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to health and resource behavior policy.

content.code:
# Evidence: k8s-worked-k8s-health-resources-qos-4
  - competency: k8s-worked-k8s-health-resources-qos-4
    artifact: health-resource-policy.yaml
    decision: worked k8s health resources qos 4
    environment: air-gapped release cluster using an internal registry mirror
    fault-injection: drop one metrics series during an autoscaling surge
    retained-proof: accessible incident timeline with causal regression evidence
    case-key: worked4
    change: |
      requests: {cpu: 200m, memory: 256Mi}
      limits: {memory: 512Mi}
      startupProbe: bounded-cold-start
      readinessProbe: traffic-contract
      livenessProbe: process-deadlock-only
      fault-test: throttling-and-oom
    verify: kubectl get pod status-api -o json && kubectl top pod status-api --containers
    failure: Reject dependency-based liveness, requests as runtime caps, green idle probes as capacity proof, or unexplained OOM and eviction.
    repair: Measure workload demand, separate probe purposes, calculate QoS, inject startup and pressure faults, and correlate container and node evidence.
content.caption: Runnable Kubernetes object and transfer-evidence contract example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

content.title: Non-worked case
content.text: Requests reserve dedicated physical resources while limits determine scheduler placement. This fails the K8s Requests Limits Scheduling Runtime contract because it omits a precondition, invariant, ownership rule, or counterexample needed by this exact case.
